title: Citrix NetScaler ADC and Gateway Critical Memory Disclosure CVE-2026-3055 (CVSS 9.3): Unauthenticated Out-of-Bounds Read via SAML IdP Configuration Leaks Session Tokens and Authentication Data — Exploitation Imminent

sources.name: Citrix Security Bulletin CTX696300 - NetScaler ADC and NetScaler Gateway Security Update
Rapid7 ETR - CVE-2026-3055 Citrix NetScaler ADC and NetScaler Gateway Out-of-Bounds Read
Arctic Wolf Security Blog - CVE-2026-3055 Citrix NetScaler
Qualys ThreatPROTECT - Citrix NetScaler ADC and NetScaler Gateway Multiple Vulnerabilities
SecurityWeek - Critical Citrix NetScaler Vulnerability Poised for Exploitation, Security Firms Warn
The Hacker News - Citrix Urges Patching Critical NetScaler Vulnerability
Security Affairs - Citrix NetScaler Critical Flaw Could Leak Data, Update Now
Help Net Security - NetScaler ADC and Gateway CVE-2026-3055
SOCRadar - CVE-2026-3055 NetScaler Memory Disclosure Analysis

timeline.event: Citrix CEO Kumar Palaniappan proactively emails customers urging them to apply forthcoming patches, signaling awareness of imminent high-severity disclosure

Citrix publishes Security Bulletin CTX696300 disclosing CVE-2026-3055 (CVSS 9.3 out-of-bounds read) and CVE-2026-4368 (CVSS 7.7 race condition/session mixup); patched versions released; CERT-EU issues advisory same day

Multiple security research firms (Rapid7, Arctic Wolf, Qualys, SecurityWeek) publish analyses warning that exploitation is imminent given historical exploitation patterns for Citrix memory disclosure vulnerabilities (CitrixBleed precedent)
No public proof-of-concept or confirmed in-the-wild exploitation yet; however, security community assessment indicates exploitation window is open and likely

simple_summary.what_happened: Citrix disclosed a critical security flaw on March 23, 2026, in NetScaler ADC and NetScaler Gateway - the network appliances that millions of enterprises use as their VPN, load balancer, and application delivery gateway. The vulnerability (CVE-2026-3055) allows an attacker to remotely read chunks of the appliance's memory without logging in first, potentially exposing session tokens, authentication data, and sensitive information belonging to legitimate users. Security researchers are warning this vulnerability follows the same pattern as 'CitrixBleed' (CVE-2023-4966), which was massively exploited in 2023.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Organizations running Citrix NetScaler ADC or NetScaler Gateway versions before 14.1-66.59 or 13.1-62.23 are vulnerable. The most severe risk applies to appliances configured as SAML Identity Providers (IdP) - organizations using NetScaler for SAML-based single sign-on are particularly exposed. NetScaler is widely deployed in financial services, healthcare, government, and large enterprises as a core network security component.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Attacker identifies a Citrix NetScaler ADC or Gateway appliance exposed to the internet, using tools like Shodan to find devices configured as SAML Identity Providers. Step 2: Without any authentication, the attacker sends specially crafted network requests to the SAML IdP endpoint, triggering the out-of-bounds memory read vulnerability. Step 3: The appliance responds with data from memory regions beyond the intended buffer boundary, which may contain session tokens, authentication cookies, private keys, or other sensitive data belonging to currently authenticated users. Step 4: Attacker uses leaked session tokens to hijack active VPN sessions or authenticated user sessions without knowing any passwords. Step 5: With hijacked sessions, attacker gains authenticated access to internal corporate resources, bypasses multi-factor authentication, and can move laterally through the network.
what_you_should_do: Immediately patch all Citrix NetScaler ADC and Gateway appliances to version 14.1-66.59 or 13.1-62.23 (or 13.1-37.262 for FIPS/NDcPP builds); treat this as emergency patching given the CitrixBleed precedent
As a temporary mitigation if patching is delayed, apply Citrix's Global Deny List (GDL) mitigation which does not require a reboot, and restrict access to SAML IdP endpoints via IP allowlisting
Check your NetScaler configuration for SAML IdP usage with: 'show authentication samlIdPProfile' - if configured, prioritize patching immediately as this configuration is required for exploitation
After patching, invalidate all active sessions to force re-authentication and prevent any already-leaked session tokens from being usable by attackers
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: NetScaler is the crown jewel of enterprise network security - it controls who gets VPN access, manages SSO authentication, and sits in front of critical internal applications. A vulnerability that lets attackers read its memory without logging in is extremely dangerous because session tokens extracted this way grant full authenticated access, bypassing all MFA protections. The precedent from CitrixBleed (CVE-2023-4966) - a nearly identical vulnerability class - showed that ransomware groups exploited it to breach dozens of major organizations within weeks of disclosure. Security firms are specifically warning this follows the same exploitation pattern.

tactics.description: Attackers exploit the out-of-bounds read vulnerability in internet-facing Citrix NetScaler appliances configured as SAML IdP to leak memory contents without authentication
The out-of-bounds read directly leaks appliance memory contents including active session tokens, authentication cookies, and potentially private key material from TLS operations
Leaked session tokens can be used to hijack active VPN and application delivery sessions, granting attacker authenticated access to internal network resources that the victim user can access
By using stolen legitimate session tokens, attackers appear as authenticated users in logs, bypassing MFA and making detection significantly harder compared to credential-based attacks

detection_guidance.summary: Detection is challenging because exploitation may not leave obvious logs, similar to CitrixBleed. Focus on monitoring for malformed SAML requests, anomalous memory access patterns, unusual session behavior (concurrent sessions from different IPs, sessions from unexpected geographies), and use of leaked tokens from unexpected source IPs.

log_sources.description: Monitor for SAML IdP authentication errors, malformed SAML requests, and unusual request patterns to /saml/ endpoints that may indicate exploitation probing
Look for concurrent sessions from different source IPs for the same user, session token reuse from unexpected IP addresses, and sudden changes in user session geolocation
WAF rules can detect and block anomalous request patterns targeting SAML endpoints; review WAF alert logs for exploitation attempt indicators
Correlate NetScaler authentication events with user behavior baselines; session hijacking typically produces anomalies like rapid location changes or unusual access times

what_to_look_for.indicator: Malformed or oversized requests to SAML IdP endpoints
what_to_look_for.description: Exploitation attempts may generate malformed SAML requests or requests with unusual size characteristics targeting the SAML IdP authentication path
what_to_look_for.log_field: url CONTAINS '/saml/' AND (request_size > threshold OR response_code IN (500, 400, 200)) AND source_ip NOT IN (known_saml_clients)

what_to_look_for.indicator: Session token reuse from multiple source IPs
what_to_look_for.description: If an attacker successfully leaks a valid session token, they will present it from a different IP than the original session owner, creating a duplicate session from two IPs simultaneously

what_to_look_for.indicator: Authenticated access from unexpected geographic locations
what_to_look_for.description: Session hijacking using leaked tokens typically originates from attacker infrastructure in different countries than the legitimate user
what_to_look_for.log_field: user_session AND geolocation != user_baseline_location AND authentication_method='session_token'

what_to_look_for.indicator: SAML IdP configuration check for vulnerable deployments
what_to_look_for.description: Proactively verify if your NetScaler is configured as SAML IdP - if yes, treat patching as highest priority
what_to_look_for.log_field: show authentication samlIdPProfile (NetScaler CLI) - any output confirms SAML IdP is configured

immediate_actions.action: Emergency Patch All NetScaler ADC and Gateway Appliances
immediate_actions.description: Upgrade to patched versions immediately: NetScaler ADC/Gateway 14.1-66.59 or later, 13.1-62.23 or later, 13.1-37.262 or later (FIPS/NDcPP). Given the CitrixBleed precedent, treat this as emergency patching. Check Citrix Downloads portal for the correct firmware.
immediate_actions.command:
# Verify current NetScaler version via CLI
show version
# Update through Citrix GUI: System > Upgrade

immediate_actions.action: Apply Global Deny List (GDL) Mitigation if Patching is Delayed
immediate_actions.description: Citrix provides a temporary GDL mitigation that restricts access to vulnerable SAML IdP endpoints. This does not require a reboot and can be applied immediately. Contact Citrix support or refer to CTX696300 for implementation guidance.

immediate_actions.action: Check for SAML IdP Configuration and Restrict Access
immediate_actions.description: Run 'show authentication samlIdPProfile' on the NetScaler CLI. If configured, restrict access to SAML IdP virtual servers via IP allowlisting to only trusted identity provider clients. This reduces attack surface while patching is planned.
immediate_actions.command:
# Check SAML IdP configuration
show authentication samlIdPProfile
# Restrict SAML endpoint access (example policy)
add ns acl BLOCK_SAML_EXTERNAL DENY -srcIP != <trusted_ip_range> -destPort 443

immediate_actions.description: After applying patches, force re-authentication for all active sessions to ensure any potentially leaked session tokens cannot be used by attackers. Monitor for suspicious re-authentication patterns that may indicate stolen token usage.
immediate_actions.command:
# Kill all active VPN sessions via NetScaler CLI
kill icaconnection -all
kill aaa session -all
